items.title: Surviving the Robot: How to Apply Lean to Automated Lines
items.summary: Topic: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) & Automation The Academic View: Lean is more about continuous improvement and mantaining the gains. The Reality: You…
items.content_html:
Topic: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) & Automation
The Academic View: Lean is more about continuous improvement and mantaining the gains.
The Reality: You will be assigned to a highly automated robotic production line with every parameter fixed. You will then realize you have no idea how it works, and somehow you will feel useless and frustrated.

It is really intimidating. The electrical engineers are talking about “CAN bus communication,” and the mechanical engineers are talking about “servo torque.”

Take a breath. You feel like you have to be omniscient and know all those obscure contents. But you don’t. Your job is to find the problem, measure the loss, and LEAD the technical TEAM to fix it. Here is how you apply lean to a machine you don’t fully understand using the TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) framework.

1. The Foundation: 5S is Actually Inspection

Look at the TPM House. The entire structure sits on one foundation: 5S. If you don’t know how the robot’s gearbox works, that’s totaly fine. But can you see if it’s leaking oil? If it’s vibrating? If it’s making noise? If it’s creating metal shavings?

On automated equipment, 5S is not just about making the machine look pretty. Cleaning is inspection.

  • Wipe down the pneumatic lines to see if there is an air leak.
  • Sweep the floor to see if a loose bolt vibrated off the conveyor. If you keep the machine to a strict 5S standard, you will catch 80% of machine failures before they break the line.

2. Autonomous Maintenance (The Operator’s Job)

The hardcore maintenance technicians are expensive and busy. They should not be wasting time changing air filters or wiping dust off sensors.

As the Lean Engineer, your job is to create visual standards for the machine operators.

  • Mark the pressure gauges with a green safe zone and a red danger zone.
  • Create a simple daily checklist, like: Check oil level, wipe Sensor A, listen for rattling. When the operator maintains the basic health of the machine, the mechanics can focus on the complex breakdowns.

3. Focused Improvement (Leading the Experts)

This is where you show your corporate value. The mechanical engineer knows how to fix the machine. But they usually don’t know what to fix and how to integrate the result into the system.

You bring the Data. Specifically, you track the OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) and search for two things:

  • The Micro-Stoppage: Automated machines rarely suffer massive, hour-long breakdowns. Instead, they suffer from 10-second minor stops (e.g., a part gets jammed, and the operator clears it and hits reset in mere seconds). This happens 50 times a shift and destroys efficiency. You track this data and show the engineers exactly which part of the machine is causing the jams.
  • The Changeover (SMED): You don’t need a robotics degree to reduce setup time. If a station/machine takes 1 hour to swap from Product A to Product B, use Single-Minute Exchange of Die. By just taking actions like organizing the tooling, staging the materials beforehand, and standardizing the process, you can cut downtime in half without knowing a line of code.

4. Why Skipped the Other Pillars

If you look at the TPM House, there are 5 other pillars (Planned Maintenance, Early Equipment Management, etc.).

Ignore them for now. As a junior engineer, you do not own the budget to schedule major planned maintenance, and you don’t have the authority to redesign the machine early equipment management. If you try to do everything, you will step on the toes of the maintenance manager and the senior engineers.

Stay in your lane. Master the foundation 5S, empower the operators (autonomous maintenance), and bring the data (focused improvement). Master those three sections, and the technical experts will follow your lead.

items.title: How to Ask 5WHYs Correctly
items.summary: Topic: Root Cause Analysis & The 5 Whys The Academic View: To find a root cause, sit down and ask yourself “Why?” five…
items.content_html:
Topic: Root Cause Analysis & The 5 Whys
The Academic View: To find a root cause, sit down and ask yourself “Why?” five times until you reach the core problem.
The Reality: If you ask the wrong “Why,” the direction will deviate and you will probably end up blaming human error or bad management. You cannot engineer a fix for either of those.

The biggest mistake novices make with the 5 Whys is focusing on the answer. But the real challenge is actually knowing how to ask a better question.

A Novice’s 5 Whys

Let’s look at a classic example of a bad 5 Whys analysis:

  • Problem: The car broke down on the highway.
  • Why 1: Why the car broke on the middle of a road?—Because the engine failed.
  • Why 2: Why the engine failed?—Because the engine oil went bad.
  • Why 3: Why the engine oil went bad?—Because I didn’t change the oil in time.
  • Why 4: Why did you forget to get an oil change?—Because I forgot to check the mileage.
  • Why 5: Why did you forget to check the mileage?—Because of poor personal planning (management/human error).

The Result: The corrective action is “Try harder to remember me next time” or “Retrain the driver.”

In the manufacturing world, all engineers share the view that manual work is always unreliable and prone to errors; so, this is pretty much a useless fix. If you submit a report like this to a customer that says “Operator forgot,” they will reject it and note you as incompetent. If your root cause relies on a human “trying harder,” the defect will definitely happen again, no matter how hard he/she tries.

How to Ask Better Questions

To do a high-quality 5 Whys analysis, you need to change how and where you ask the questions:

Rule 1: Ban the words “Forgot,” “Careless,” and “Management.” If an operator forgot a step, do not ask “Why did they forget?” That always leads to psychology and objection. Instead, ask: “Why did the production system allow the following process to continue even after a step was completely missed?” And that leads to engineering.

Rule 2: Focus on Physics and Process, not Psychology. Always direct the questions back to the physical mechanics of the workstation, the wear on the tooling, or the rigid logic of the system.

Rule 3: Split the Problem in Views of Occurrence vs. Escape You can run two separate tracks of Whys:

  1. Occurrence: Why did the machine make the bad part?
  2. Escape: Why did our inspection fail to catch it before it shipped?

Rule 4: Never do it at your desk. You cannot ask “Why?” to a white board. You must go to the gemba—the factory floor. Look at the broken part, watch the machine cycle, and ask the operator. If you keep guessing the answers in a conference room and are too busy or too introvert to walk on the field, you will fix the wrong problem.

The Professional 5 Whys

Let’s re-do the car example using the mindset.

  • Problem: The car broke down from engine failure.
  • Why 1: Why the car broke in the middle of a road?—The oil lost its viscosity and failed to lubricate the cylinders. (Physics)
  • Why 2: Why the oil lost its viscosity?—The oil remained in the car 5,000 miles past its operational limit. (Process)
  • Why 3: Why the car could still drive after passing the mile limit by so much?—The driver was not notified that the limit was reached. (System)
  • Why 4: Why there was no notification?—The car does not have a sensor to track mileage and alert the user automatically. (Design)

The Result: The corrective action is to install a maintenance light on the dashboard that triggers to alert the driver.

The Takeaway

If your 5 Whys end with management problem or operator error, you have stopped too early. People are flawed. They will always forget, get tired, and make mistakes. So don’t blame people, focus on process and physics.

items.title: Building a Production Line from Scratch
items.summary: Topic: New Product Introduction (NPI) & Line Setup The Academic View: You are given a list of tasks with perfect cycle times, and…
items.content_html:
Topic: New Product Introduction (NPI) & Line Setup
The Academic View: You are given a list of tasks with perfect cycle times, and you draw a perfectly balanced U-shaped cell workstation on paper.
The Reality: You are given only a 3D model (and sometime your computer’s crappy 3D viewer exhausts you, because the company only spends money on good high-end software licensing only for the design team), half the parts are missing, the cycle times are guesses, the floor has occupied by existed products and production starts in three months.

If you are a new Industrial Engineer assigned to an NPI team, setting up the physical line can be overwhelming. Here is a roadmap for how a line actually comes to life, and the paperwork you need to build it.

Phase 1: The Document Cascade

You cannot draw a physical floor plan until you know what you are building. In NPI, documents (we will only discuss documents related to industrial engineering at this time) are generated in a strict sequence. One feeds the next.

  1. The BOM (Bill of Materials): Tells you what parts you need.
  2. The Process Flow Diagram (PFD): Based on the BOM, you map out the steps you need to take. (e.g., Step 1: Screw base. Step 2: Attach wire. Step 3: Test).
  3. The PFMEA & Control Plan: Quality reviews the PFD to decide where things might fail. This tells you exactly where you must place inspection and testing stations on the physical line.
  4. The Time Study: You estimate or measure how long each step takes.
  5. The Line Balance: You group the steps together to meet the customer’s Takt Time. This tells you how many operators and workstations you need.
  6. The Physical Line Layout.

Rule of Thumb: Never start drawing the physical layout in AutoCAD until the Process Flow and Line Balance are drafted.

Phase 2: The Evolution of a Line

You do not build the final mass-production line on Day 1. The layout scales with the product’s maturity phases:

  • Prototype Stage: No line is needed. Engineers are just building the product on a workbench, or even on his own desk, to see if it turns on, that is the only purpose.
  • EVT / DVT (Engineering & Design Validation): You build a temporary line. It will be ugly and messy. You use folding tables, rolling carts, and PVC pipes in the lab. You are simply proving out the assembly sequence and figuring out what tools are required.
  • PVT (Production Validation): This is the pilot line. You lock down the space in the real jobshop and physical layout, assign operators to specific seats, and run at production speed to prove your cycle times.
  • Mass Production (SOP): The line is fixed, and your job shifts from “Setup” to continuous improvement.

Phase 3: Balancing Perfection

The biggest mistake young engineers make is trying to design a 100% perfect line before a single part is mass-produced.

During EVT and PVT, the product design will change. An operator will complain that a tool is too heavy. A cycle time you estimated at 10 seconds will actually take 30.

  • Do not bolt the tables to the floor.
  • Design the initial layout to be 80% good then it is enough.
  • Put your workstations and material racks on caster wheels.
  • Leave empty space between stations so you can add buffer inventory or extra test equipment later when things go wrong.

items.title: How to Make a Concession Like a Pro
items.summary: Topic: Quality Judgments & Concessions The Academic View: Specifications are black and white. Good parts ship. Bad parts are scrapped. Nobody can change…
items.content_html:
Topic: Quality Judgments & Concessions
The Academic View: Specifications are black and white. Good parts ship. Bad parts are scrapped. Nobody can change nothing.
The Reality: Manufacturing lives in the gray zone. If you scrap every part with a microscopic anomaly, the profit margin of the product would be eaten up easily.

Sometimes, you have to make a concession (accepting a part that deviates slightly from the perfect standard, as said in ISO9001), balance the cost and risk like a pro.

1. The Golden Rule: Form, Fit, and Function

When you find a deviation, ask yourself one question: Does this affect Form, Fit, or Function (FFF) of the product?

  • Internal Concession: If the defect is strictly cosmetic (e.g., a scratch on a surface), it does not affect FFF. You can document the deviation, approve it internally, and ship it and you have saved the company some money like a senior engineer, who knows how to protect the business. But remember, sometime cosmetic is the fuction, if a part’s face is used at the outer side of a final product and final customers can see it easily.
  • External Concession: If the deviation changes how the part fits or works, you must ask the client for a formal waiver. Never hide a functional defect. That is a breach of contract.

2. The Undefined Defect

What happens when you find a weird defect that isn’t even listed in your SIP or could be found in PPAP documents? You have to solve it and create the standard on the spot for the parts that has been produced.

  1. Find the absolute worst-case part that you still believe is acceptable.
  2. This physical part becomes your boundary sample, or limit sample.
  3. Sign it, date it, and place it at the inspection station. Now the operators aren’t guessing; they are comparing.

No matter what, an internal report to your superior is always required and knowledge sharing between your colleagues is in favor.

However, it is a little bit tricky on communication strategy to the client about this matter. If the undefined defect affects the FFF, or the related term has a higher FMEA score, you should always ask the client for a formal approval. And you always ought to prepare the client rejecting your request without a reason or doing you a favor, simply saying: “The PSW has signed and PPAP has frozen. It is your problem.”

3. Judging the Borderline in a Batch Context

When dealing with a minor cosmetic issue, you have to judge the context of the whole batch. Clients usually accept a tiny percentage of minor cosmetic flaws (called an Acceptable Quality Limit, or AQL).

  • Healthy Batch: If the rest of the batch is running perfectly, you can afford to be slightly lenient and internally concede a few borderline cosmetic parts.
  • Sick Batch: If the process is unstable and the batch is already close to the rejection limit, your buffer is gone. Every borderline part must be strictly scrapped to protect the shipment.

4. The Complaint Tax Dynamic Standards

Here is the secret they don’t teach in school: The “passing score” is dynamic.

Let’s say your standard inspection strictness is a 90/100 in a mathmatical view. If the client called you yesterday screaming about a quality issue, your new passing score has definitely become higher, like to 95/100 for example.

  • Why? Because the client is currently hyper-sensitive. They will inspect your next shipment with a magnifying glass.
  • A borderline defect that made your last month in peace is an immediate “Scrap” this month. You must temporarily over-inspect to rebuild trust.

The Takeaway

Making a concession is not cheating. It is calculated risk management.

  1. Protect Form, Fit, and Function above all else.
  2. Let the context of the batch dictate how you handle the gray area.
  3. Adjust your strictness based on the client’s current trust level.

items.title: Stop Being a Janitor: How to Master the 6S Audit
items.summary: Topic: 5S Implementation & Auditing The Academic View: 5S (Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) + Safety is about organizing the workplace.
items.content_html:
Topic: 5S Implementation & Auditing
The Academic View: 5S (Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) + Safety is about organizing the workplace.
The Reality: Most new engineers treat 5S audits like a cleaning inspection. And beside that, they are not familiar to the process and procedures. They walk around, check a box if the floor is swept, and leave. This does nothing.

A clean factory can still be a dangerous, inefficient factory. As a novice, you need to stop looking at the dirt and start looking for the band-aids.

Here is a methodology for a real 6S audit in a more head-friendly way.

Level 1: The Most Fundamental Safety Check

Before you look for organization, look for immediate threats.

  • The Fire Trap: Is there cardboard or wood sitting next to a heat source (drying oven, welding bot)?
  • The Trip Wire: Are air hoses or extension cords running across the walkway?
  • The “Jury Rig”: Look for duct tape. For example, if you see duct tape holding a guard or a sensor in place, that is a safety violation disguised as a “temporary fix.”
  • Etc.

Level 2: The Band-Aid Hunt

This is where people separate the novices from the pros. A novice sees a crumpled plastic bag stuffed into a gap in the machine and thinks: “Trash. 5S violation.” A pro sees the crumpled bag and thinks: “Why is that there?”

  • The Leak: Is the bag/rag there to catch an oil leak?
  • The Vibration: Is it wedged there to stop a panel from rattling?
  • The Sensor: Is it blocking a sensor to force the machine to run?

The Rule: If you see trash inside or on a machine, it is usually hiding a mechanical failure. Cleaning is also inspection. Think beyond just picking it up; find the leak.

Level 3: The Struggle Check

Stand at the station and watch the operator does his job for a few cycles. Do not look at the SOP papers first; look at their hands.

  • The Search: Do they have to turn around, walk away, or dig through a bin to find a wrench?
  • The Reach: Are they reaching over a moving part to grab a bolt?
  • The Reset: When they are done, do they have a specific place to put the tool back, or do they just set it on the table?

If the operator has to think about how to start and finish a motion, it failed the set-in-order check.

Level 4: The Ghost Check

Look at the shadow board or the tool outline.

  • Empty Shadows: If there is an outline for a hammer, but no hammer, where is it? Is the operator using a heavy wrench instead because the hammer is missing?
  • Ghost Tools: Are there tools on the desk that don’t have a place for reset? This means the process changed, or negelected, but the 5S didn’t keep up.

The Takeaway

Don’t walk around with a clipboard looking for dust bunnies. You are not a maid. You are hired to be an engineer, a detective! If you fix the root cause of an issue, the mess would be gone forever. If you just tell the line leader to clean it up, things will be back tomorrow.

items.title: Industry 4.0 is a Trap. Meet Squirrel 4.0.
items.summary: Topic: Automation Strategy vs. Reality The Academic View: Industry 4.0 is the future. “Lights Out” factories with AI and robots will replace human…
items.content_html:
Topic: Automation Strategy vs. Reality
The Academic View: Industry 4.0 is the future. “Lights Out” factories with AI and robots will replace human labor and solve all quality issues.
The Reality: If you automate a mess, you just get a faster and more expensive mess.

I learned this from watching them trying to automate the Barbie line.

A Million-Dollar Lesson

At the time, the company was aggressively pushing Industry 4.0 ideology. The goal was to cut human labor to cut costs. They installed complex, fully automated production lines from a 3rd party company to assemble the dolls.

These expensive robots trying to do the job was a catastrophe.

  1. Low Flexibility: Barbie fashions change every month. Humans can switch from Beach Barbie to Astronaut Barbie in seconds. However, the robots needed expensive reprogramming for every dress change.
  2. Frequent Downtime: When a human drops a doll, they pick it up and discard it. When a robot drops a doll, it jams the machine, stops the line, and requires a technician to fix it and a whole set of reset procedure.
  3. Cost: The depreciation and maintenance of the robots cost more than the labor they replaced.

Eventually, they had to scrap the automation plans and brought the humans back.

Enter Squirrel 4.0

The engineers and managers realized they couldn’t leap from manual to full auto without bridging. So the board launched a project playfully named: Squirrel 4.0.

The Philosophy: Just as a squirrel gathers nuts one by one to prepare for winter, engineers gather small, standardized improvements one by one (actually with a monthly quota, lmao) to prepare for automation.The ideology changes to: you cannot automate what you cannot stabilize.

How It Works

Instead of buying expensive robots, the team focused on LCIA (Low Cost Intelligent Automation).

Phase 1: The Human Sensor They kept the operators but standardized their movements strictly. They identified exactly where the pain points were.

  • The “Nut” includes every small kaizen, every standardized work instruction, every error-proofing device.

Phase 2: Partial Automation They didn’t replace the operator; they gave them better tools.

  • Example: Instead of a robot arm placing a screw, they installed a simple gravity-fed chute (costing around \$20) that delivers the screw to the operator’s handside without moving his head.
  • Result: Cycle time dropped, consistency went up, but the human flexibility remained.

Phase 3: Full Automation Only after the process was 100% stable—as they had stored enough nuts—did they look at robots again. By then, they knew exactly what the machine needed to be because the humans had already perfected the motion.

items.title: Amoeba Management: Turning Employees into Business Owners
items.summary: Topic: Financial Motivation & Organizational Structure The Academic View: Lean is about eliminating waste to improve flow. Everyone cooperates because it optimizes the…
items.content_html:
Topic: Financial Motivation & Organizational Structure
The Academic View: Lean is about eliminating waste to improve flow. Everyone cooperates because it optimizes the whole system, creating the result of 1 + 1 > 2.
The Reality: Most employees are not motivated by system optimization. They are motivated by their paycheck.

If you want to understand why some departments fight over pennies, or why your bosses, like managers or directors, refuse to give some grace to help another team, you need to understand Amoeba Management (or “Profit Center” management).

What is it?

In a traditional company, only the CEO worries about Profit & Loss (P&L). In Amoeba Management, created by Kyocera, the company is sliced into tiny business units—Every department acts like a separate small business.

  • The Machining Dept sells parts to the Assembly Dept.
  • The Assembly Dept sells finished goods to Sales.
  • The Maintenance Dept charges an hourly rate to fix machines, serving all production teams.

Why it Drives Lean

This system turns employees into business owners.

1. Kaizen = Cash in Pocket In a normal company, if you save \$100,000 through improvements, the money disappears into the corporate banking account that you have no access to. You get a “Thank You” email, maybe with a pizza party. In this system, that \$100,000 stays on your department’s P&L. It directly funds your team’s year-end bonuses.

  • Result: You don’t have to beg anyone to do lean. They hunt for waste aggressively because waste is literally stealing money from their bonus.

2. The “Internal Customer” is Real Lean teaches: “The next process is your customer.” Usually, this is just a metaphor. But under amoeba management, it is literal. If you send a defective part to the next department, they will refuse to accept the whole batch and not pay a dime, and you are legally required to send out people to their jobshop to do 100% sorting.

  • Result: Quality isn’t enforced by inspectors; it is enforced by the wallet.

The Bad: The “Silo” Trap

There is still a bad side. When every department fights for their own profit, they stop caring about the whole company, or cooperation.

1. The Transfer Price War A quality crisis has happened, and since multiple departments share the responsibility, each must bear a portion of the associated costs. However, managers typically engage in fierce, bitter disputes over how these costs should be allocated—conflicts that frequently escalate all the way to the GM.

2. Innovation Hoarding If your team finds a better way to run a machine, you might keep it secret to maintain your advantage over other departments. This kills company-wide improvement. The board recognized this issue quite early on; typically, they organize experience-sharing sessions at the corporate level. Consequently, this is a manageable.

The Takeaway

You might not be able to change your company’s accounting system. But you must understand the mindset:

Engineering decisions are financial decisions.

  • If your company treats departments as profit centers, expect fierce internal competition.
  • If your company treats departments as cost centers, expect budget restrictions.

Learn how your boss is measured. If you save them money in the way they are measured, you become their most valuable asset.

items.title: Translating Geek to Business: From Cpk to DPPM
items.summary: Topic: Process Capability & Risk Assessment The Academic View: Calculate $C_{pk}$. If the result is $>1.33$, the process is capable. The Reality: Your…
items.content_html:
Topic: Process Capability & Risk Assessment
The Academic View: Calculate $C_{pk}$. If the result is $>1.33$, the process is capable.
The Reality: Your big boss and the finance team do not care about the number “1.33.” It has nothing to do with their knowledge and dues. They care about risk and costs.

You need to stop reporting just the index number and start translating it into DPPM (Defects Per Million).

Why Convert?

In a boardroom, no one asks beside quality and project teams, “What is the standard deviation within the subgroup?” They ask: “How many bad parts are probably there?”

According to the American Society for Quality and standard six sigma textbooks, there is a direct mathematical link between process capability ($C_{pk}$) and the probability of a defect.

  • $C_{pk}$ = How well your process fits inside the goal specification limits.
  • Sigma Level = The statistical robustness of the process.
  • DPPM = The predicted number of failures per million parts produced.

The Cheat Sheet—Short Term Capability

Based on standard statistical tables (assuming a centered, normal distribution), here is the translation you need to memorize.

Cpk Value Process Sigma DPPM (Defects Per Million) The Reality
0.67 2.0 $\sigma$ 45,500 Disaster. You are scrapping ~4.5% of your product.
1.00 3.0 $\sigma$ 2,700 The Danger Zone. 0.27% defect rate. In the automotive industry, this is unacceptable.
1.33 4.0 $\sigma$ 63 The Industry Standard. You drop from thousands of defects to just 63.
1.67 5.0 $\sigma$ 0.57 World Class. Less than 1 defect per million.
2.00 6.0 $\sigma$ 0.002 Six Sigma. Perfection.

How to Use This in a Meeting

Don’t just say: “Our Cpk is 1.0.”

Say: “Our Cpk is 1.0. This predicts we will produce 2,700 defects per million parts. At our current volume, that is X bad parts per week. We need to improve the process to 1.33 to reduce that risk.”

So remember, $C_{pk}$ is not just a badge of honor for the PPAP file. It is also a financial prediction. Learn to speak the language of defects, and management will listen to your engineering proposals.

items.title: The Hidden ROI: Why Operator Happiness is an Engineering Problem
items.summary: Topic: The Human Element of Lean The Academic View: Operators are resources. You calculate their cycle time and ergonomics. If the math works,…
items.content_html:
Topic: The Human Element of Lean
The Academic View: Operators are resources. You calculate their cycle time and ergonomics. If the math works, the line runs.
The Reality: Operators are the gatekeepers of your success. If they hate the culture, your process will fail, no matter how perfect your engineering is.

1. The 8th Waste

Engineers look at data; Operators look at reality.

An operator stands at that machine for 8 to 12 hours a day. You stand there for 15 minutes.

  • The Gap: They might not know the engineering terms like OEE or Six Sigma.
  • The Gold: They know exactly which jig is sticky, which tool is too heavy, and which sensor glitches every day at 2:00 PM.
  • The Loss: If you ignore them because you feel hard to communicate with them with your “master mind”, you have committed the 8th waste: non-utilized talent. You miss the root cause, or go into a detour to find the root cause, because you didn’t ask the only person who sees it.

2. The Danger of Malicious Compliance

If an operator feels disrespected, they will completely unsubscribe themselves from the corporate dramas. They stop caring at all.

  • Scenario: A machine is drifting slightly out of spec, or creating a new characteristic that they do not seem before.
  • The Respected Operator: Stops the line, notifies the QC team, calls you, and prevents a defective product outflow.
  • The Disrespected Operator: Says, “Not my problem. The boss didn’t ask me. Or just fire me. I don’t care.” They let 500 bad parts pass through.

Later, when you face a client complaint, you will wonder why your controls failed. They failed because the human element bypassed them and you cannot engineer a safeguard against apathy.

Furthermore, you cannot tell the client the truth, such as that the operators deliberately let the defective products out because the working environment was too toxic. This will ruin the business.

3. The New Hire Tax

Lean relies on stability. High turnover is an enemy of stability, no matter how well your process has developed to be, as long as humans are invovled.

When an operator quits because the work environment is toxic, you will pay a heavy tax.

The Learning Curve Math:

  • Day 1: A new hire usually runs at 2.0x cycle time (50% efficiency).
  • Day 2-3: They improve to 1.8x cycle time or 1.5x cycle time.
  • Week 2: They might finally hit standard rate.

If you have high turnover, your line is permanently running at ~80% efficiency simply because you are always spending time on training someone new and waiting them to grow.

4. The Method: How to bridge the gap

You are the Engineer. It is your job to bridge the gap.

  • Ask “Yes or No,” not “Why”: Don’t ask “Why are you doing it that way?” It might sound accusatory. Ask this instead “Is the document design process too difficult?” This is a good topic to start. This immediately gives them the impression that you are targeting the process, not the people themselves; consequently, they are even more willing to share their views with you.
  • Fix the Pebble in the Shoe: If they complain about a flickering light, a sharp edge on a bin, or a missing mat—fix it, as long as their demands are reasonable, even though they sound less impactful. You can build good bonds with the on-site workers, and they will be more willing to report to you and provide basic assistance.

The Takeaway

You can have the best control plan in the world, but if the person pushing the button wants you to fail, you will fail. Treat operators like the subject matter experts, as they are.

items.title: A $1,000 Per Minute Nightmare: Line Stoppages & The Air Freight
items.summary: Topic: Quality Crisis Management The Scenario: A quality defect is found. All inventory at the client and your warehouse is quarantined. You now…
items.content_html:
Topic: Quality Crisis Management
The Scenario: A quality defect is found. All inventory at the client and your warehouse is quarantined. You now have zero inventory, whether at home, or at the client’s site.

Part 1: The Cost of Silence

In the automotive industry, a line stoppage means the ultimate failure. Production lines are so continuous that if one station stops, the whole factory stops. There is basically no re-routing or sub-routing in production flow management to bypass an issue and give you some space to breathe.

  • The Fine: Penalties often range from \$1,000 to \$10,000 per minute.
  • The Logistics: Even if you have the part in your hand at the client’s plant gate, it might take 20+ minutes just to walk through a massive plant, like Tesla or Daimler-Benz, to reach the line. And that walk alone already costs $20,000+ in fines.

Part 2: A Death Spiral Math

When inventory hits zero completely, most new engineers think: “I just need to rush parts there today.” However, you are also facing a pipeline problem, especially if the supplier and client are in different countries, or even different continents.

Example:

  • Customer Demand: 10 parts/day.
  • Your Max Capacity: 12 parts/day.
  • Transit Time (Marine Shipping, the Good Old Way): 30 days.

If you max out your own production capability and produce 12 parts today, you must air freight them by DHL or UPS immediately to keep the line running tomorrow.

And you can’t stop there.

Because the cheaper option—marine shipping takes 30 days, you must air freight your daily production every single day for 30 days until the first boat arrives, or you have to find a way to build up inventories at client’s site by air shipping extra products .

The Result: You are paying premium freight for a whole month. This can easily cost more than 50% of the total failure cost, especially if products are transitioning from the South & East Asia manufacturing hubs to America and Mexico. It can wipe out the profit margin of the product for months.

Part 3: Possible Solutions

We cannot always move the whole factory from a low-cost country, like China or Vietnam to the US or right in the client’s country. Some business models would not work in some other countries, and it is a multifactorial problem.

But there still are some ways to mitigate the risk.

1. The Final Assembly Node
Keep the complex, and yet labor-intensive fabrication in the low-cost country.
Move the final, simple steps, like packaging, labeling, light assembly to a location near the client, e.g., a US subsidiary or a Mexico border facility by setting up subsidiary companies.

  • Routine: Ship semi-finished parts via cheap ocean freight to the local node.
  • Crisis: If a defect is found, your inventory is already local. You can purge, rework, or finish new parts in hours, not weeks. And even you can send out your own people to the client’s site for sorting and rework, avoiding expensive 3rd party sorting and rework services.

2. The Forward Stocking Location (3PL)
If you can’t build another factory, rent a warehouse. Use a third-party logistics provider near the client to hold a few weeks of safety stock.

  • Lean Counter-point: Yes, Lean says inventory is waste.
  • Business Reality: Paying for 2 weeks of storage is a non-value-added acitivity But paying for 4 weeks of air freight is disastrous. You should balance the cost and risk with your own calculation.

3. Redundant Tooling If you cannot move assembly, you must have redundant toolings. Don’t keep all your molds and dies in one factory. If a mold breaks or a quality issue is tied to a specific machine, you need a backup set of tooling ready to run at a different location immediately.

The Takeaway

Supply chain optimization isn’t just about the lowest piece price. It is about recovery speed, or, in another term, resillience. If your factory is 30 days away from your client, you are one quality spill away from erasing product’s profit margin of the year.
